coredata.dc:title: L-tyrosine methyl ester-stabilized carbon dots as fluorescent probes for the assays of biothiols
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Haishuang
Wang, Erkang
Li, Jing
Wang, Jin
coredata.dc:description: Over the past few decades, assays of biothiols had attracted much attention due to the essential role they played in human physiology, especially using the fluorescent analysis. In most cases, competitive mechanism was often employed, where the metal ions were often introduced as the quenchers and thiols competed with metal ions due to the high binding affinity and strong thiophilicity for 'signal-on' assays. To develop a metal ions-free approach for the assays of thiols, here, L-tyrosine methyl ester capped carbon dots (Tyr-CDs) were employed and prepared as the fluorescent probes. The as-prepared Tyr-CDs displayed narrow size distribution and distinct blue fluorescence with high quantum yield (12.9%) compared with the unmodified CDs. Moreover, Tyr-CDs exhibited higher quenching efficiency due to the efficient energy transfer between Tyr-CDs and the quinone products in the presence of tyrosinase. When the targeted biothiols was present, the catalytic reaction of the tyrosinase to the formation of quinone was inhibited and the fluorescence signal was recovered in a biothiols-concentration-dependent manner, which provided the basis for the analysis of biothiols. The practical application of the present system was demonstrated by testing the biothiols in human plasma samples and good recovery was obtained, indicating that the sensing platform we proposed hold great promise in the accurate detection of biothiols in complex biosystems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biothiols analysis
Fluorescence
Metal ions-free
Tyr-CDs
Human plasma

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantitative detection of biothiols in plasma
Analytical performance of the proposed probes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China , [Grant No, 91430217 ] and the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST) of China [Grant No, 2016YFA020320 ] and [Grant No, 2013YQ170585 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) TEM and B) HRTEM images of Tyr-CDs with corresponding diameter distribution histogram (inset). C) FTIR spectra and D) XRD patterns of Tyr-CDs.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of (a) Tyr-CDs, (b) 30 μM Cu2+, (c) 30 μM benzoquinone, (d) 30 units/ml tyrosinase; (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of (a) CA CDs, (b) 30 μM tyrosinase with excessive L-tyrosine methyl ester. F0 and F represent the normalized fluorescence intensity of CA in the absence and presence of tyrosinase; (C) The fluorescence emission spectra of (a) Tyr-CDs, (b) 5 μM Cys without tyrosinase, (c) 5 μM Cys in the presence of 35 units/ml tyrosinase, (c) 35 units/ml tyrosinase.

(A) The fluorescence recovery efficiency variation with reaction time when incubated Tyr-CDs with 15 units/ml tyrosinase and 20 μM Cys; (B) The effect of pH on fluorescence of Tyr-CDs with tyrosinase (35 units/ml) and Cys (20 μM) in different pH PBS buffer; (C) The fluorescence recovery efficiency of Tyr-CDs, 5 μM Cys in the presence of different concentration of tyrosinase. Biothiols were incubated with tyrosinase for 6 min at 37 °C, then Tyr-CDs were added into the solution and incubated for another 15min before recording. (D) The fluorescence emission spectra of Tyr-CDs; 5 μM GSH, 5 μM Cys, 5 μM Hcy in the presence of 35units/mL tyrosinase, respectively. Biothiols were incubated with tyrosinase for 6 min at 37 °C, then Tyr-CDs were added into the solution and incubated for another 15 min before recording. The error bars represented triple measurements.
The fluorescence intensity of Tyr-CDs in the presence of 5.0 μM Cys, GSH, Hcy and other various amino acids. The error bars represented triple measurements.

Fluorescence emission spectra of Tyr-CDs in the presence of different biothiols concentrations (10−6 mol/L) (A) GSH: (a) 0; (b) 0.1; (c) 0.3; (d) 0.5; (e) 1; (f) 3; (g) 5; (h) 35 units/ml tyrosinase, (B) Cys: (a) 0; (b) 1; (c) 3; (d) 5; (e) 10; (f) 20; (g) 30; (h) 50; (i) 35 units/ml tyrosinase, and (C) Hcy: (a) 0; (b) 5; (c) 10; (d) 20; (e) 30; (f) 50; (g) 35 units/ml tyrosinase The insets showed relative fluorescence intensity (Fa/F0), where Fa and F0 were the fluorescence intensity of Tyr-CDs in the presence and absence of biothiols at 455 nm, respectively, versus the semi-log of the concentrations of added biothiols. The error bars from the inset of the figures represented triple measurements.
Schematic illustration of the interaction among Tyr-CDs, tyrosinase and biothiols.
Determination of biothiols in human plasma samples using Tyr-CDs.

head.ce:title: L-tyrosine methyl ester-stabilized carbon dots as fluorescent probes for the assays of biothiols

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui, 230029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Physics and Applied Mathematics, State University of New York at Stony Brook, Stony Brook, New York, 11794-3400, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics, Jilin University, Changchun, Jilin, 130012, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Over the past few decades, assays of biothiols had attracted much attention due to the essential role they played in human physiology, especially using the fluorescent analysis. In most cases, competitive mechanism was often employed, where the metal ions were often introduced as the quenchers and thiols competed with metal ions due to the high binding affinity and strong thiophilicity for 'signal-on' assays. To develop a metal ions-free approach for the assays of thiols, here, L-tyrosine methyl ester capped carbon dots (Tyr-CDs) were employed and prepared as the fluorescent probes. The as-prepared Tyr-CDs displayed narrow size distribution and distinct blue fluorescence with high quantum yield (12.9%) compared with the unmodified CDs. Moreover, Tyr-CDs exhibited higher quenching efficiency due to the efficient energy transfer between Tyr-CDs and the quinone products in the presence of tyrosinase. When the targeted biothiols was present, the catalytic reaction of the tyrosinase to the formation of quinone was inhibited and the fluorescence signal was recovered in a biothiols-concentration-dependent manner, which provided the basis for the analysis of biothiols. The practical application of the present system was demonstrated by testing the biothiols in human plasma samples and good recovery was obtained, indicating that the sensing platform we proposed hold great promise in the accurate detection of biothiols in complex biosystems.

Schematic illustration of the interaction among Tyr-CDs, tyrosinase and biothiols.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Biological thiols, including homocysteine (Hcy), cysteine (Cys), and glutathione (GSH), play pivotal roles in human physiology due to the typical redox features [1]. A high level of Hcy than normal can promote oxidation and aging of the lipoprotein, weaken the immune system, leading to issues of brain damage etc [2]. In clinical practice, the level of Hcy (H valve) is used as the indicator of heart disease and apoplexy. Deficiency of Cys may lead to the issues of slow growth, liver damage, edema, skin lesions and so on [3]. GSH, as a tripeptide combination of glycine, Cys and glutamic acid, is most abundant in the cell and can be seen in almost all biological process such as liver protection, detoxify effect, anti-allergic and anti-aging process. Moreover, it protects thiol group of Cys in proteins from being oxidized [4]. Thus it is crucial to detect biothiols in biosystems to diagnose underlying diseases according to their wide distribution and fluctuation level.

Over the past few years, bunches of methods have been explored to test biothiols, such as capillary electrophoresis [5], high performance liquid chromatography [6], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [7], etc. However, complicated instruments and separation steps were often invovled when the above-mentioned analytical methods were used to achieve the determination of biothiols. As a comparision, fluorescence analysis with fast response, high sensitivity and easy operational simplicity aroused great interest. To date, many fluorescent assays on detection of biothiols based on nanoprobes have been developed [8,9]. In most cases, the principles of fluorescent assays of detecting thiols were based on the competive mechanism for signal-on sensing, where the metal ions were often introduced as the quenchers and thiols competed with metal ions due to the high binding affinity and strong nucleophilicity [10,11]. The commonly quenchers mainly focused on the involvement of heavy metal ions, such as Hg2+, Ag+ and Cu2+ [8,11,12], which limited the wide application. Therefore metal ions-free sensing strategy with high quenching-efficiency fluorescent nanopores should be developed for the analysis of biothiols.

Carbon dots (CDs) as a class of a 'zero dimensional' heavy metal-free carbon materials, have been the subject of many researches owing to their tunable optical properties, chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility, small size and low cost [13]. The outstanding spectral and photophysical properties endowed them great potential in the bioimaging [14], drug delivery [15] and biosensing [16]. Similar to the other nanoprobes, when CDs was used for the detection of biothiols, heavy metals ions were often introduced as the quenchers [12]. To aviod the usage of heavy metal ions and improve the quenching efficiency between donors of CDs and quenchers, herein, L-tyrosine methyl ester was introduced onto the surface of the CDs to prepare Tyr-CDs and the corresponding sensing platform was constructed to detect biothiols (Scheme 1 ). The fluorescence of Tyr-CDs displayed intense blue emission at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm and could be acted as the efficient optical donor. In the presence of tyrosinase, the functionalized units of L-tyrosine methyl ester on the surface of the Tyr-CDs could be transformed into the quinone, which quenched the fluorescence signal efficiently through the energy transfer between Tyr-CDs and the quinone products [17]. When biothiols was introduced, they would interact with the Cu2+ sites of tyrosinase and led to the inactivation of tyrosinase. Therefore, the fluorescence of the Tyr-CDs would recover and the recovered trends was dependent on the concentration of biothiols. The notable fluorescence variation was applied for sensitive and selective detection of biothiols in human plasma samples. The sensing platform presented was simple, environment-friendly and cost-effective, holding a promising potential for further practical biothiols analysis.

Citric acid (CA), Na2HPO4, KH2PO4, NaCl, KCl and other salts were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works (China). L-tyrosine methyl ester and tyrosinase were obtained from Yuanye Bio-Technology Co. Ltd (China, Shanghai). GSH was bought from Acros Organics (New Jersey, USA). Cys and other amino acids were got from Alfa Aesar. Hcy was purchased from Sigma. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiment. All the reagents and chemicals were of analytical pure without further purification. Ultrapure water was purified by Millipore system (18.2 MΩ cm−1). PBS solution was prepared and the final concentration was 0.1 M.

UV-vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectroscopy were recorded on Cary 60 UV-vis (Agilent Technologies, USA) and Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies), respectively. TEM and HRTEM were obtained by a JEM-2100F high-resolution transmission electron microscope (Netherlands) operated at 200 kV. Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was collected on a Thermo Nicolet-380 IR spectrophotometer (USA) with the KBr pellet technique ranging from 400 to 4000 cm-1. Powder X-ray Diffraction (XRD) was collected with a D8 ADVANCE (Germany) using Cu Ka radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained from an ESCALAB-MKII X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (VG Scientific, UK). Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) images were acquired through a multimode nanoscope V scanning probe microscopy system (Bruker, USA). The samples for AFM test were prepared by spreading the diluent aqueous suspension of Tyr-CDs on a freshly cleaved mica surface and placed to be dried naturally.

The Tyr-CDs were prepared via a direct prolyzing CA [18] and an incomplete carbonization in the presence of L-tyrosine methyl ester. The synthetic process was different from the previous work [17], where hydrothermal reaction was adopted. Here, CA (0.5 g) and L-tyrosine methyl ester (0.25 g) were mixed and heated to 200 °C for 25 min. The reactants begun to melt in 2 min, accompanied with the color change from light brown to deep brown during this process. Subsequently, the mixed solution was cooled down to the room temperature naturally. 10 mL water was added into the above mixture to obtain the dispersive Tyr-CDs. Finally, the solution was adjusted to be neutral by NaOH and stored at room temperature for further use. The resulting brown solution was centrifuged for XPS measurement. And brown powder obtained by freeze-dried approach was used for further characterization. As a control, the CDs without modification was prepared with prolyzing CA under the same condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of biothiols in plasma
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application was performed using deproteined plasma samples since biothiols usually bound with proteins [19]. Triphenylphosphine was added as the catalyst to prevent the formation of disulfuide bouds, and acetonitrile was used to precipitate proteins. The supernatant was collected by centrifugation. When we used the present platform for the determination, sample was firstly diluted with 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) to be consistent with the dynamic range of our assay. And then, the diluted sample was mixed with fluorescent Tyr-CDs. To prove the presence of the biothiols in plasma samples, maleimide was introduced for blocking reagent of the biothiols as a control. Fluorescence recovery studies were carried out subsequently and the total biothiols concentrations were determined by standard addition method.

The formation of the Tyr-CDs via pyrolysis and the incomplete carbonization was firstly confirmed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the resulting Tyr-CDs exhibited excellent dispersibility, and the inserted size distribution image revealed that the average size of the dots was 2.2 nm. The HRTEM image depicted in Fig. 1B showed that the lattice spacing was 0.21 nm, which was almost in agreement with the (100) crystal facet of graphite [17,20]. To investigate the thickness of the as-prepared Tyr-CDs, AFM measurement was employed (Fig. S1). It manifested that the height of the most of Tyr-CDs was 2–3 nm, corresponding to 3–5 layers of graphite [21]. To further confirm the successful functionalization of L-tyrosine methyl ester on the CDs through pyrolysis, FTIR spectra were also recorded. As depicted in Fig. 1C, the typical absorption peak of 3406 cm−1 and the stretching vibration band of C=O at 1703 cm−1 confirmed the existence of –COOH and –OH etc. The absorption peaks in 3028 cm−1, 1403 cm−1 corresponded to C-H bending vibration of methyl or incompletely reacted CA [22]. The C=C stretching vibration at 1516 cm−1, 1610 cm−1 indicated the existence of the aromatic functional group of L-tyrosine methyl ester. Furthermore, XRD pattern was also employed to examine the crystal structure. A peak centered at 21.3° in XRD spectrum with the interplanar distance of 0.42 nm was assigned to (002) crystal face of graphite. The interlayer spacing was larger than the theoretical value (0.34 nm) due to the presence of oxygen-contained groups, which was also observed with that obtained using hydrothermal reaction [17]. XPS was also investigated to reveal the molecular structure (Fig. S2) and all of the typical peaks of the C1s, N1s and O1s appeared. As showed in the spectrum of C1s, the characteristic peaks of 289.0 eV, 286.8 eV, 285.9 eV, 285.6 eV, 284.4 eV after deconvolution were ascribed to binding energy of carbon in C=O,C-O,C-N,C-C,C=C, respectively [22,23]. In the spectrum of O1s, the peaks at 532.6 eV, 534.2 eV appeared, which belonged to the binding energy of C=O and C-O [22]. The N1s spectrum can be fitted by three subpeaks with the binding energy 400.6 eV, 401.4 eV, 402.5 eV, corresponding to C-N, C3-N, N-H [22,23], All the above results indicated the successful functionalization of L-tyrosine methyl ester on the surface of Tyr-CDs. In addition, the subsequent fluorescence quenching experiments also confirmed the presence of the L-tyrosine methyl ester on the surface of Tyr-CDs without oxidation.

The optical properties of as-synthesized Tyr-CDs was also investigated using UV-vis absorption and fluorescence spectrum. The as-prepared Tyr-CDs displayed bright blue light under 350 nm irradiation (Fig. S3A insert). As shown in Fig. S3, an absorption peak at around 275 nm in the UV-vis spectra was corresponded to the p–p* transition of nanocarbon [24]. The fluorescence spectra (Fig. S3B) showed that the obtained Tyr-CDs exhibited strong emission at 455 nm under the excitation wavelength at 350 nm. The quantum yield of Tyr-CDs was determined to be 12.9% using quinine sulphate as standard (Fig. S6), which was higher than that obtained of CDs (9.0%) without modification [17] and the samples via a hydrothermal reaction (3.8%). The fluorescence features of Tyr-CDs at the excitation wavelength from 330 nm to 400 nm were further investigated. Excitation wavelength-independent behavior of Tyr-CDs was observed, which was beneficial for the eliminating the effect of the background in the recording process, especially in the biological system. Therefore, the nanoprobes were employed for the sensing biothiols and Scheme 1 demonstrated the sensing principle. Here the quinone generated in the presence of tyrosinase was used as the quencher. As well known, tyrosinase was a copper-containing enzyme and biothiols can interact with the Cu2+ sites of the tyrosinase and therefore inhibit the activity of tyrosinase, which led to fluorescence signals recovery. The recovered fluorescence signal was dependent on the concentration of the biothiols, which provided the basis for the assays for the biothiols. As we known, in the most previous works, Cu2+ was used often as the quencher of fluorescent nanoprobes [25], so the effect of Cu2+ was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the incubation of Tyr-CDs with 30 μM Cu2+ could not lead to the fluorescence change, indicating Cu2+ had no influence on the fluorescence in the present system. While the addition of benzoquinone led to obvious quenching of the fluorescence. The trend was similar to that caused by incubation with the tyrosinase, which further confirmed that the quenched fluorescence was from the quinone formed on the surface of Tyr-CDs. Notably, as observed in Fig. 2B, the quenching efficiency with Tyr-CDs (ca. 70%) was enhanced compared with that using unmodified ones (30%) due to the efficient energy transfer between the quinone molecular of the surface of nanoprobes and donor of Tyr-CDs. To confirm the feasibility of sensing, Cys was chosen as a model and the fluorescence intensity variations after incubation with tyrosinase were collected. Here, to make sure Cys and tyrosinase can react completely, they were mixed together before adding Tyr-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2C, no change was observed with the individual presence of Cys. However, when tyrosinase existed, the addition of Cys led to the fluorescence signal recovery, demonstrating that the enhanced fluorescence signal was from the effect of Cys on the activity of the tyrosinase.

As described above, the incubation of Tyr-CDs with enzyme led to the quenching of fluorescence, while thiol-containing compounds could react with enzyme resulting in the recovery of fluorescence. Therefore, the incubation condition was very important for obtaining high performance. Firstly, the incubation time of Tyr-CDs with tyrosinase (15 units/ml) was firstly optimized. The fluorescence quenching of Tyr-CDs occurred immediately and completed in 15 min (Fig. S4). Therefore, 15 min was chosen as the incubation time of tyrosinase and Tyr-CDs at 37 °C. Moreover, the incubation time between enzyme and biothiols was also investigated. Taking Cys as the model, the incubation time between tyrosinase and biothiols was also studied. We found that the fluorescence recovery efficiency (R%=(F
a
-F)/(F
0
-F) × 100%, Where F
0 and F represented fluorescence intensity of Tyr-CDs at 455 nm in the absence and presence of tyrosinase, respectively. F
a was the recovered fluorescence intensity at 455 nm) reached the maximum and kept almost unchanged when the incubation time was 6 min with 15 units/mL tyrosinase and 20 μM Cys. The pH had a great influence on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs [12,13], and thus the pH effect on the fluorescence recovery efficiency was carried out by adding 20 μM Cys into 0.1 M PBS buffer at various pH. As shown in Fig. 3 B, the recovery efficiency at 455 nm increased in the acid media from 4.4 to 7.4. With the further pH increase to 9.4, negligible effect on the fluorescence recovery efficiency was observed. However, when switching to the alkaline environment (eg. pH = 12.4), the recovery efficiency decreased sharply. To get the good sensitivity and maintain the bioactivity of tyrosinase, pH 7.4 was chosen for the subsequent experiments. Moreover, the influence of the enzyme concentration was also studied with the incubation of Cys. The recovery efficiency of fluorescence intensity decreased gradually with the increased amount of tyrosinase (Fig. 3C). Although the lower limit of detection (LOD) was obtained with the low concentration tyrosinase, the dynamic linear ranger was narrower. To obtain a wider linear range and lower LOD, 35 units/ml of tyrosinase was chosen finally. The response to different biothiols using Tyr-CDs under the optimal condition was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 3D, the same concentration of biothiols was added into the solution, and the recovery efficiency order follows: GSH > Cys > Hcy, which was similar to the results obtained before [26]. It resulted from the decreased steric hindrance effect which was caused by the tyrosinase-bithiols complex due to the biothiols would prevent the subsequent catalytical reaction between tyrosinase-bithiols complex and the unit of L-tyrosine methyl ester on the surface of Tyr-CDs to form the quenchers of quinone.

The specificity of the present probes toward the biothiols was evaluated with 21 types of amino acids. The fluorescence spectra before and after the addition of these amino acids were measured. Obviously, remarkable enhancement in the fluorescence signal was noticed in the presence of Cys, GSH and Hcy, whereas nearly no variation was detected with others (Fig. 4 ). The high selectivity was due to the fact that tyrosinase had a strong affinity with thiol-containing amino acids through S-Cu bonds which weaken the catalytical activity of tyrosinase, and thus inhibited the formation of quinone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the proposed probes
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the good selectivity of the present sensing platform, we investigated the analytical performance of the as-prepared probes under the optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 5 , with the increase of the targets concentration, the fluorescence intensity was recovered and exhibited a good linear relationship between Fa/F0 and log Cbiothiols, which was similar to the previous work on previously reported work [27]. The obtained plots showed good linear relationships within the range from 1 × 10−6 to 5 × 10−5 mol/L (R2 = 0.994) for Cys, 5 × 10−6 to 5 × 10−5 mol/L (R2 = 0.964) for Hcy, 1 × 10−7 to 5 × 10−6 mol/L (R2 = 0.987) for GSH, respectively. The detection limits of Cys, Hcy and GSH were 1.2 × 10−7 mol/L, 3.5 × 10−6 mol/L, and 3.1 × 10−8 mol/L, respectively.

The practical application of the present platform was demonstrated using human plasma samples. As shown in Fig. S5, using deproteined plasma samples without the treatment, the fluorescence intensity of Tyr-CDs was greatly recovered. To confirm the presence of biothiols, the fluorescence response of Tyr-CDs incubated with the treated samples via thiols-blocking reagents of maleimide for 2 h was collected. No obvious fluorescence enhancement was observed, indicating the fluorescence intensity change was caused by biothiols in the plasma. Standard addition method was performed and Cys was chosen as the model biothiol because of its high concentration in human plasma [19] for quantitative recovery. Before measurement, the plasma samples were diluted to ensure the concentration of the biothiols in the predefined linear range. The obtained concentrations of biothiols in three human plasmas were listed in Table 1 , and the recovery of known amount Cys in the plasma samples ranged from 90.80% to 118.50%, indicating the reliability of Tyr-CDs for detecting biothiols in biological fluids.

L-tyrosine methyl ester functionalized CDs with enhanced quantum yield were prepared by pyrolysis using CA as precursor and employed as the efficient optical probe for the assays of biothiols. The synthesis procedure of our probe was simple and economical, avoiding the sophisticated synthetic process. The functionalized units of the L-tyrosine methyl ester on the surface of the CDs were efficiently oxidized to form quinone through tyrosinase-catalyzed reaction and the fluorescence of Tyr-CDs can be quenched. Compared with the unmodified ones, the Tyr-CDs showed much higher quenching efficiency in the presence of tyrosinase due to the efficient energy transfer. Moreover, the present system avoided the inputs of heavy metals as quenchers, which was beneficial for the bioanalysis. Given the strong thiophilicity of Cu2+ to the biothiols, the Tyr-CDs were successfully applied in the detection of total biothiols in the plasma samples. The present system with high sensitivity and fast response provided a powerful tool to detect biothiols in the practical application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, [Grant No, 91430217] and the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST) of China [Grant No, 2016YFA020320] and [Grant No, 2013YQ170585].

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2017.12.014.
